transcript: We're going to head to Thailand now and take a look at air pollution there. 10 million people were thought to have sought treatment for illnesses linked to air pollution last year. A report looked at cases like chronic conditions, lung cancer, bronchitis, asthma, heart disease. Panisa Amosha told us more from Bangkok. Air pollution has become a seasonal thing for us Thai people. So every now and again we expect that during the drought, so probably around December to about April, that we will face with this kind of pollution. Last year on 20 and 23, Bangkok enjoyed good, not polluted day only for 31 days of the year. And other provinces, especially toward the north of Thailand, for example Chiang Mai, they suffered a lot, not only for the hotspot that coming from Thailand, but also from other country as well. In 2019, pollution has become a national agenda. And we're trying to solve the problem. But so far, if you look into the outcome, so we have the number from 2023. There are three measures that the government set that whether or not it achieved to solve the problem. So on the number of the day that we are polluted or not, the number has been increased from 26 day in 20 and 22 to 52 day in 20 and 23. This is the first measure. The second measures on the hotspot. Within the country, it has been increased from about 50,000 spot in 20 and 22 to 170,000 hotspot in last year. And last but not least about the patient. We have recorded of 20 and 22 patients with lung cancers, disease in respiratory, about 900,000. And now it's up to 2.2 million. So, so far, the national agendas from 2022. And 19 has been failed.
